content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 31,
1992
Bill Baber
4167 46th Street
San Diego,
CA
92105
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1-92-121
Dear Mr.
Baber:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
!
Because your questions are
general in nature,
we are treating your letter as a request for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (4) •2/
Your letter, dated February 13,
1992,
accurately summarizes
my advice that the definition of “intermediary” does not include a
person who receives checks made payable to a candidate’s committee
and who forwards those checks to the committee treasurer.
(Regulation 18432.5,
copy enclosed.
3
!)
I also advised you that
the requirement contained in Section 84305 to include the name and
address of the sender of a mailing is not applicable to any
mailing of fewer than 200 substantially similar pieces sent in a
calendar month.
Section 84305 requires such identification on any
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
3w”
Regulation 18432.5 was amended by the Commission on July 9,
1991, to remove from the definition of “intermediary” persons who
merely transmit contributions to a candidate or committee.
The
amendment has not yet gone into effect.
However, the enclosed
version represents the current policy of the Commission.
428 J Street, Suite SO •
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 9804-08O7
•
(UÔ) 322-660


Bill Baber
1—92—121
Page
2
“mass mailing,” which is defined as over 200 substantially similar
pieces of mail.
(Section 82041.5.)
If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
tJdLA)
By:
Carla Wardlow
Acting Division Chief
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division
Enclosure

sections.question: 1) Does the definition of 'intermediary' include a person who receives checks made payable to a candidate's committee and forwards those checks to the committee treasurer? 2) Does the requirement in Section 84305 to include the name and address of the sender of a mailing apply to mailings of fewer than 200 substantially similar pieces sent in a calendar month?
sections.conclusion: The definition of 'intermediary' does not include a person who receives checks made payable to a candidate's committee and who forwards those checks to the committee treasurer. The requirement contained in Section 84305 to include the name and address of the sender of a mailing is not applicable to any mailing of fewer than 200 substantially similar pieces sent in a calendar month.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor seeks clarification on whether a person who receives and forwards checks to a candidate committee qualifies as an 'intermediary' under campaign finance law, and whether the sender identification requirement for mailings applies to mailings under 200 pieces.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A person who merely transmits contributions to a candidate or committee does not meet the definition of 'intermediary,' and the sender identification requirement for mailings only applies to 'mass mailings' of over 200 substantially similar pieces.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is informal advice letter (not opinion/formal written advice). Contains clear question and conclusion sections. References regulation amendment not yet in effect. Some OCR artifacts present but text is legible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) Does the definition of 'intermediary' include a person who receives checks made payable to a candidate's committee and forwards those checks to the committee treasurer? 2) Does the requirement in Section 84305 to include the name and address of the sender of a mailing apply to mailings of fewer than 200 substantially similar pieces sent in a calendar month?

CONCLUSION: The definition of 'intermediary' does not include a person who receives checks made payable to a candidate's committee and who forwards those checks to the committee treasurer. The requirement contained in Section 84305 to include the name and address of the sender of a mailing is not applicable to any mailing of fewer than 200 substantially similar pieces sent in a calendar month.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms informal telephone advice provided to Bill Baber regarding the definition of 'intermediary' and mailing identification requirements under the Political Reform Act. The advice clarifies that check forwarders are not intermediaries and that mailing identification is only required for mailings exceeding 200 pieces.
